role_summary: Dentist; adult adviser to the NAACP Youth Council in St. Augustine, Florida; president of the SCLC Florida branch; survivor of a September 1963 KKK kidnapping and beating; invited Martin Luther King Jr. to St. Augustine in spring 1964, the campaign that helped produce the Civil Rights Act of 1964.
ai_reading: Hayling's top semantic neighbor is C. T. Vivian (entry 134) at a high cosine count of 220, more than double his next neighbor; the catalog's clearest SCLC-direct-action pairing outside the Wyatt Tee Walker / Vivian dyad. The pairing fits the cultural record (Hayling presided over Florida SCLC and brought King into the St. Augustine campaign that drew Vivian and other SCLC leadership) but the embedding count is notably high for an interviewee whose own cultural placement is regional and dental-professional rather than SCLC-headquarters. His other neighbors are the joint Hamilton/Ulmer interview (entry 4), Robert L. Carter (entry 108, NAACP LDF), Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan (entry 51), and H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, Mississippi community health). His axis positions are extraordinarily centrist: all five are within +/-0.18 of zero, matching the same vector-centrism pattern visible in McDew (entry 16), Terry (entry 40), and Richardson (entry 49). For a survivor of a documented KKK kidnapping and beating, the embedding's near-zero placement across all axes is the novel observation: the violent specificity of the September 1963 incident does not pull his testimony toward any pole, possibly catching the dentist's clinical-professional register more than the survivor's pain-narrative register.
biographical_paragraph: Hayling was the first African American dentist elected to the local, regional, and national components of the American Dental Association [src: 2]. As adult adviser to the NAACP Youth Council in St. Augustine he led protests against segregation during the city's 400th-anniversary commemorations of 1965 [src: 2]. In September 1963 he and three companions (James Jackson, Clyde Jenkins, and James Hauser) were kidnapped and taken to a Ku Klux Klan rally, where they were severely beaten [src: 2]. He served as president of the Florida branch of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference and invited Martin Luther King Jr. to St. Augustine in spring 1964; the campaign that followed was pivotal for the passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 [src: 2]. He was inducted into the Florida Civil Rights Hall of Fame in 2014; St. Augustine's largest park has since been named in his honor [src: 2]. Born November 20, 1929 in Tallahassee, Florida, Hayling died December 20, 2015 in Fort Lauderdale at eighty-six [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 103 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
movement_context: Hayling moved to St. Augustine in 1960 to open a dental practice on Bridge Street; within months he had taken on the role of adult adviser to the local NAACP Youth Council, a position he held through the central organizing years of 1960 to 1964 [src: 3][src: 4]. The Youth Council under his guidance ran sit-ins at the segregated lunch counters of St. Augustine's downtown commercial corridor, organized picketing of the Ponce de Leon Hotel and the Plaza de la Constitucion, and confronted the city's preparations for the federally funded 1965 quadricentennial celebration; Hayling argued that no federal money should subsidize a celebration that excluded the city's Black residents [src: 3][src: 5]. On September 18, 1963 Hayling, dental colleague Clyde Jenkins, contractor James Hauser, and pharmacist James Jackson were abducted from a Ku Klux Klan rally near St. Augustine that they had been observing from concealment; the four were tied to a stake, beaten with chains and a tire iron, and doused with kerosene before sheriff's deputies arrived and dispersed the assembly [src: 5][src: 6]. The four men's beating drew national press coverage and is documented in Colburn's Racial Change and Community Crisis and Branch's Pillar of Fire as a precipitating event that pulled SCLC headquarters into the city [src: 3][src: 4]. In early 1964 Hayling, then serving as president of Florida SCLC, issued a formal invitation to Martin Luther King Jr. and SCLC staff to make St. Augustine the spring 1964 campaign target; King, Andrew Young, and Hosea Williams arrived in late May, opening a sustained six-week direct-action phase that included night marches into Plaza de la Constitucion, kneel-ins at segregated white churches (the same tactical pattern St. Augustine organizer Barbara Edna Vickers, catalog entry 5, participated in), and the wade-ins at St. Augustine Beach and the Monson Motor Lodge pool [src: 4][src: 5]. On June 11, 1964 King and Ralph Abernathy were arrested on the steps of the segregated Monson Motor Lodge restaurant on the St. Augustine bayfront, the only arrest of King's career inside Florida [src: 4][src: 7]. On June 18, 1964 Monson manager James Brock poured muriatic acid into the motel pool while integrated demonstrators were swimming together, the photograph (carried by the Associated Press and the Tampa Tribune) widely credited in the historiographical record with shifting Senate Republican Minority Leader Everett Dirksen's stance on cloture [src: 3][src: 4]. The cloture vote ending the seventy-five-day filibuster against the civil rights bill was held the following day; the bill cleared the Senate June 19 and was signed into law by President Johnson on July 2, 1964 [src: 4]. The youth foot-soldier cohort Hayling had assembled through the NAACP Youth Council included teenage participants like Purcell Maurice Conway (catalog entry 99) and Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan (catalog entry 51), whose later oral histories ground the St. Augustine campaign's youth-base structural pattern [src: 6][src: 8].
legacy_and_reception: Hayling moved his dental practice to Cocoa Beach, Florida in 1966 after his St. Augustine office on Bridge Street was firebombed and his liability insurance was canceled; he continued to practice dentistry in Cocoa Beach for more than three decades, retiring in the late 1990s [src: 5][src: 6]. The St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument, dedicated May 14, 2011 in the Plaza de la Constitucion, lists Hayling among the thirty-one foot soldiers named on its central plaque alongside the Youth Council and adult organizers he had recruited and led [src: 5][src: 9]. The ACCORD Civil Rights Museum, opened in St. Augustine in 2014, occupies the restored Bridge Street building that housed Hayling's original dental office; the museum's permanent exhibits center the September 1963 Klan abduction, the 1964 SCLC campaign, and the Youth Council's organizing infrastructure [src: 5]. Hayling was inducted into the Florida Civil Rights Hall of Fame in 2014; in 2015 the city of St. Augustine renamed its largest municipal park as Dr. Robert B. Hayling Freedom Park in his honor [src: 2][src: 5]. He died December 20, 2015 in Fort Lauderdale at the age of eighty-six; his funeral at St. Paul AME Church in St. Augustine was attended by Andrew Young, the Florida congressional delegation, and the surviving St. Augustine Youth Council members [src: 5][src: 6]. His Library of Congress oral history, recorded September 14, 2011 by interviewer Joseph Mosnier as part of the Civil Rights History Project's Florida fieldwork, is catalogued at the American Folklife Center as accession AFC 2010/039 and at the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture as accession 2011.174.46 [src: 1].

gallery.caption: St. Augustine civil rights demonstrations, 1964, from the Florida Memory project of the State Library and Archives of Florida. The footage documents demonstrators at Butler Beach, integrated occupation of city streets, and intervention by the Florida Highway Patrol during the spring 1964 SCLC direct-action campaign Hayling organized as president of Florida SCLC.
gallery.photographer: State Library and Archives of Florida (Florida Memory project)

gallery.alt: James Brock pouring muriatic acid into the Monson Motor Lodge swimming pool, June 18, 1964
gallery.caption: Monson Motor Lodge manager James Brock pouring muriatic acid into the motel's swimming pool during the June 18, 1964 swim-in by integrated demonstrators, photographed by Vernon Barchard for the Tampa Tribune. The image, widely reproduced by the Associated Press, is the most-cited single photograph from the St. Augustine campaign Hayling and Florida SCLC had organized, and is credited in the historiographical record with shifting Senate Republican Minority Leader Everett Dirksen's cloture position on the civil rights bill.
gallery.license: Public domain (US, published 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

gallery.alt: St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument plaque, dedicated May 14, 2011
gallery.caption: The St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument plaque in the Plaza de la Constitucion, dedicated May 14, 2011. Hayling is among the thirty-one foot soldiers' names inscribed on the central plaque, alongside the Youth Council and adult organizers he led through the 1960-1964 St. Augustine campaign.
